questions.question: Differentiate between distance and displacement.
questions.answer: Distance is the total path travelled by an object, while displacement is the shortest distance between the initial and final positions of an object.

questions.question: Explain uniform motion with an example.
questions.answer: Uniform motion occurs when an object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time. Example: a train moving at constant speed on a straight track.

questions.question: Explain non-uniform motion with an example.
questions.answer: Non-uniform motion occurs when an object covers unequal distances in equal intervals of time. Example: a car moving in traffic.

questions.question: Define speed and write its formula.
questions.answer: Speed is the distance travelled per unit time. Formula: Speed = Distance / Time.

questions.question: What is velocity? Give one example.
questions.answer: Velocity is the speed of an object in a specific direction. Example: a car moving at 60 km/h towards the north.

questions.question: Explain acceleration with an example.
questions.answer: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time. Example: a car speeding up when the driver presses the accelerator.

questions.question: What is uniform acceleration?
questions.answer: Uniform acceleration occurs when the velocity of an object changes by equal amounts in equal intervals of time.

questions.question: What is retardation?
questions.answer: Retardation is negative acceleration that decreases the velocity of an object.

questions.question: Explain what a distance-time graph shows.
questions.answer: A distance-time graph shows how distance travelled by an object changes with time.

questions.question: What information does the slope of a distance-time graph give?
questions.answer: The slope of a distance-time graph gives the speed of the object.

questions.question: Explain what a velocity-time graph represents.
questions.answer: A velocity-time graph represents the change of velocity of an object with time.

questions.question: What does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?
questions.answer: The area under a velocity-time graph represents the displacement of the object.

questions.question: Write the three equations of motion.
questions.answer: v = u + at, s = ut + ½at², and v² − u² = 2as.

questions.question: State the conditions under which equations of motion are applicable.
questions.answer: The equations of motion are applicable when the motion is along a straight line with uniform acceleration.

questions.question: What is meant by uniform circular motion?
questions.answer: Uniform circular motion is the motion of an object along a circular path with constant speed.

questions.question: Why is circular motion considered accelerated motion?
questions.answer: Because the direction of velocity changes continuously even though speed remains constant.

questions.question: Name the SI unit of speed and acceleration.
questions.answer: Speed: metre per second (m/s); Acceleration: metre per second squared (m/s²).

questions.question: Explain average velocity.
questions.answer: Average velocity is the displacement divided by total time taken.

questions.question: Give one difference between speed and velocity.
questions.answer: Speed is distance per time while velocity is displacement per time with direction.

questions.question: What does a horizontal line in a velocity-time graph indicate?
questions.answer: It indicates that the object is moving with uniform velocity.

questions.question: Name the instrument used to measure vehicle speed.
questions.answer: Speedometer.

questions.question: Name the instrument used to measure distance travelled by a vehicle.
questions.answer: Odometer.

questions.question: Give two examples of uniform circular motion.
questions.answer: Motion of Earth around the Sun and motion of a satellite around Earth.

questions.question: What does the slope of a velocity-time graph represent?
questions.answer: The slope represents acceleration.

questions.question: Explain why motion depends on reference point.
questions.answer: Motion is relative because an object may appear moving with respect to one reference point but stationary with respect to another.
